title: Rust hello world and variables
summary: Learn how a simple Rust program prints text and stores values in variables.
goal: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to print text in Rust and create simple variables.
explanation: Rust can look more strict than beginner languages, but the first steps are still manageable when taken slowly.
println! is a Rust macro that prints text to the terminal. It works a bit like print() or console.log().
let creates a variable. In early Rust, think of it as naming a value so you can use it again later.
codeNotes: fn main() is the starting point of the Rust program.
println! prints a line of text.
{} is a placeholder that Rust fills with the value of learner_name.
commonMistakes: Leaving out a semicolon after a statement.
Using the variable name differently later, such as learnerName instead of learner_name.

exercise.title: Introduce a learner in Rust
exercise.instructions: Create a variable for a learner name and print a sentence that says they are starting Rust on Coding Hub.

title: Rust mutability basics
summary: Learn the difference between a normal Rust variable and a mutable one.
goal: By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Rust protects variables from being changed unless you allow it.
explanation: In Rust, variables are not changeable by default. This is called immutable by default.
If you want to change a variable later, you add mut after let.
This rule helps beginners and experienced developers avoid accidental changes in code.
codeNotes: let mut completed_lessons creates a variable whose value can change.
The first println! shows the starting number.
The second println! proves the variable changed successfully.
commonMistakes: Trying to change a variable that was created without mut.
Forgetting a semicolon at the end of a statement.
Misspelling the variable name when updating it.

exercise.title: Track lesson progress
exercise.instructions: Create a mutable variable for completed lessons, print the first value, update it, and print the new value.
